meta.prompt: Refresh — added WHY descriptions for foundation, umbrellas, and storage after design review session

title: Architecture Overview
subtitle: How an AppySentinel is composed — and why each layer exists

layers.label: Foundation — Baked In
layers.description: Every always-on headless process faces the same seven unavoidable problems. These primitives solve them once, consistently, so recipe authors never have to. Remove any one and the Sentinel becomes unsafe to run unattended.

items.name: Signal Envelope
items.description: Without a shared data shape, nothing can subscribe generically. This is the contract every collector emits against and every store/transport receives. OTel-aligned so signals are portable to external observability systems.

items.name: SignalBus
items.description: Without internal pub/sub, collectors would call storage and transports directly — tight coupling. Adding a new store would mean touching every collector. The bus decouples them: emit once, any number of subscribers receive.

items.name: Lifecycle
items.description: Every process receives SIGTERM eventually — OS shutdown, restart, deploy. Without structured shutdown, buffered data is lost and files may be corrupted mid-write. SIGHUP enables config reload without restart.

items.name: ConfigLoader
items.description: Hierarchical config: built-in defaults → config file → env vars. Zod-validated. Reloadable on SIGHUP.

items.name: AtomicWrite
items.description: Temp-file + rename pattern. Crash-safe flat-file writes.

items.name: SerialQueue
items.description: Multiple collectors writing concurrently to the same file will race and corrupt JSONL. The queue serialises writes without blocking callers — they enqueue and move on.

items.name: Logger
items.description: A headless process is invisible without structured logging — you cannot attach a debugger or watch its output interactively. Pre-configured means consistent log format across all recipes without each one setting up Pino differently.

layers.label: Collect — data flows IN
layers.description: The Sentinel reaches out and grabs data, or listens for data arriving. Inbound only. The question answered: what does this Sentinel need to know about, and how does it find out?

items.name: poll-command
items.description: Run a local command on a pulse interval. Use when the data source is a CLI tool or script.

items.name: poll-http
items.description: HTTP GET on a pulse interval. Use when the data source is an HTTP API.

items.name: orchestrator-ssh
items.description: SSH multi-host poll via bash-over-stdin. Zero remote footprint — nothing installed on target machines. The AppyRadar pattern: one Sentinel collects from five machines.

items.name: sql-diff-collector
items.description: Poll a database by updated_at watermark, write changed records to a local JSON mirror. Use when the source is a DB and you want a local queryable copy without hitting production.

items.name: watch-directory
items.description: File system events via chokidar. Use when data arrives as files.

items.name: watch-logfile
items.description: Tail a log file with rotation detection. Use when the source writes structured or semi-structured log lines.

items.name: hook-receiver
items.description: Inbound webhook listener. Use when an external system pushes events to the Sentinel.

items.name: subprocess-wrap
items.description: Supervised long-running subprocess. Use when the data source is a process you need to own and stream output from.

items.name: snapshot-capture
items.description: Combine signals from multiple collectors into one structured document at the end of a collection cycle.

layers.label: Internal — Storage & Enrichment
layers.description: What happens to data after collection and before it is exposed or delivered. The core question: is this data about what IS (current state) or what HAPPENED (history)?
layers.storage_rule: File-based is the default. SQL introduces schema migrations, installation configuration, and multi-machine sync complexity — all support nightmares for a droppable-on-any-machine tool.

items.name: jsonl-store
items.description: Append-only JSONL + index JSON. Choose this when data is a history — events that accumulate over time and you may want to replay or query back through.

items.name: snapshot-store
items.description: Single overwriting JSON file, replaced each collection cycle. Choose this when data is current state — you want the latest picture, not a history.

items.name: memory-buffer
items.description: Ephemeral ring buffer, no persistence. Edge case — for high-frequency signals where only the last N records matter and history has no value.

items.name: sqlite-store
items.description: Non-default. Must earn its place per case. Justified only when query complexity cannot be served by file reads.

items.name: event-normaliser
items.description: Ensures all collected signals conform to the full Signal envelope before storage — fills in defaults, validates schema.

items.name: classifier (tiers 1–3)
items.description: Enrichment pipeline: rules-based (fast, deterministic) → regex/heuristic → LLM semantic. Each tier adds meaning to raw signals.

layers.label: Expose — Sentinel is readable
layers.description: The Sentinel makes itself queryable. Others come to it and pull what they need. Primarily local — Sentinels are not internet-facing services. The consumer is typically a local AI agent, developer tool, or dashboard on the same machine or tailnet.
layers.open_decision: Expose is currently read-only by default. Whether it should also accept writes (local control surface) is an open design decision — write capability belongs in this umbrella as an opt-in, not a default.

items.name: api-expose
items.description: HTTP REST via Hono. The foundation surface — reachable by any HTTP client, browser, or script. Start here.

items.name: cli-expose
items.description: Pipe-friendly shell interface. For local dev composition (pipe to jq, grep) and on-machine agent loops.

items.name: mcp-expose
items.description: MCP server. Native surface for AI agent consumers. The right choice when the primary consumer is a Claude or other MCP-aware agent.

layers.label: Deliver — data flows OUT
layers.description: The Sentinel pushes data outward to downstream systems without being asked. Use this when data needs to reach a place the consumer cannot query directly — a central aggregator, a cloud store, an observability platform.

items.name: http-push
items.description: Batched HTTP POST with retry and backoff. The general-purpose outbound transport.

items.name: file-relay
items.description: rsync / Syncthing / network mount. Use when the destination is another machine on the same network or tailnet.

items.name: supabase-push
items.description: Push signals into a Supabase table. Use when a cloud-accessible store is needed.

items.name: otlp-push
items.description: Translate Signals → OTLP and push to an OTel collector. Use when integrating with existing observability infrastructure.

items.name: socketio-emit
items.description: Push to a remote Socket.io server. Deliver role only — the Sentinel is a client here, not a server.

layers.label: Operational — Always-on supervision
layers.description: A Sentinel is only useful if it keeps running. These recipes register the Sentinel as a managed process so it starts at boot, restarts on crash, and can be controlled by the host OS.

items.name: register-as-launchd
items.description: macOS launchd plist. The right choice for all machines in the AppyDave fleet.

items.name: register-as-systemd
items.description: Linux systemd user service. For Linux servers and remote machines.

items.name: register-as-pm2
items.description: PM2 ecosystem file. Cross-platform fallback when OS-native supervision isn't available.

items.name: register-as-docker
items.description: Dockerfile + compose. For containerised or cloud deployments.
